content:
# Acts Chapter 25 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 256-257 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_025_page_001.jpg, ACT_025_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Festus therefore, having arrived in the province, after three days went up to Jerusalem from Caesarea. **2** And the chief priests and the leading men of the Jews brought charges before him against Paul, and they were urging him, **3** asking a favor against him, that he would summon him to Jerusalem — plotting an ambush to kill him along the way. **4** Festus then answered that Paul was being kept in custody at Caesarea, and that he himself was about to depart shortly. **5** "Therefore," he said, "let those among you who are in authority come down with me, and if there is anything wrong with this man, let them bring charges against him."

**6** And having spent among them not more than eight or ten days, he went down to Caesarea, and on the next day he sat upon the judgment seat and commanded Paul to be brought. **7** And when he arrived, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem stood around him, bringing many and serious charges which they were not able to prove, **8** while Paul said in his defense, "Neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have I sinned in anything."

**9** But Festus, wishing to grant the Jews a favor, answered Paul and said, "Are you willing to go up to Jerusalem and there be judged before me concerning these things?" **10** But Paul said, "I am standing before the judgment seat of Caesar, where I ought to be judged. To the Jews I have done no wrong, as you also very well know. **11** If then I am a wrongdoer and have committed anything worthy of death, I do not refuse to die; but if none of the things are true of which these men accuse me, no one can hand me over to them. I appeal to Caesar." **12** Then Festus, having conferred with his council, answered, "To Caesar you have appealed; to Caesar you shall go."

**13** And when some days had passed, Agrippa the king and Bernice arrived at Caesarea, greeting Festus. **14** And as they were spending many days there, Festus laid Paul's case before the king, saying, "There is a certain man left behind as a prisoner by Felix, **15** concerning whom, when I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and the elders of the Jews brought charges, asking for a sentence of condemnation against him. **16** To whom I answered that it is not the custom of Romans to hand over any man before the accused has met his accusers face to face and has been given opportunity to make his defense concerning the charge."

**17** "When therefore they had come together here, I made no delay, but on the next day I sat upon the judgment seat and commanded the man to be brought. **18** Concerning whom, when the accusers stood up, they brought no charge of the evil things that I was expecting, **19** but they had certain disputes with him about their own religion and about a certain Jesus who had died, whom Paul affirmed to be alive. **20** And being at a loss as to how to investigate these matters, I asked whether he wished to go to Jerusalem and there be judged concerning these things. **21** But when Paul appealed to be kept in custody for the decision of the Augustus, I commanded him to be kept until I could send him up to Caesar."

**22** And Agrippa said to Festus, "I myself also was wishing to hear the man." "Tomorrow," he said, "you shall hear him."

**23** So on the next day, when Agrippa and Bernice had come with great pomp and had entered the audience hall, together with the commanders and the prominent men of the city, at the command of Festus, Paul was brought in. **24** And Festus said, "King Agrippa and all you men who are present with us, you see this man about whom the whole multitude of the Jews petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here, crying out that he ought not to live any longer. **25** But I found that he had committed nothing worthy of death, and when he himself appealed to the Augustus, I decided to send him. **26** Concerning whom I have nothing definite to write to my lord. Therefore I have brought him before you all, and especially before you, King Agrippa, so that after the examination has taken place, I may have something to write. **27** For it seems unreasonable to me, in sending a prisoner, not also to indicate the charges against him."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "the province" (τη επαρχεια):** Sinaiticus reads τη επαρχεια, referring to the Roman province of Judea. Rendered "the province" to preserve the administrative terminology of the Greek.

2. **v.6 — "eight or ten days" (ου πλειους οκτω η δεκα):** Sinaiticus reads "not more than eight or ten days," a characteristically Lukan expression of approximate time. Some manuscripts read "not more than ten days"; the Sinaiticus reading with the doubled numeral is preserved here.

3. **v.8 — "sinned" (ημαρτον):** Sinaiticus uses the verb αμαρτανω ("to sin/miss the mark") rather than a more general word for wrongdoing. This is theologically significant — Paul frames his defense not merely in legal terms but in terms of sin against Torah, Temple, and imperial authority.

4. **v.10 — "judgment seat of Caesar" (βηματι Καισαρος):** The βημα is the official tribunal or raised platform from which Roman magistrates rendered judgment. Rendered "judgment seat" consistently throughout Acts (cf. 18:12, 25:6, 25:17) to maintain the juridical weight of the term.

5. **v.11 — "I appeal to Caesar" (Καισαρα επικαλουμαι):** The technical legal term επικαλεομαι ("I call upon / appeal to") marks the formal provocatio ad Caesarem — the right of a Roman citizen to have his case heard by the emperor. This is the pivotal legal moment of the narrative.

6. **v.19 — "religion" (δεισιδαιμονιας):** This word can range from "religion" (neutral) to "superstition" (pejorative). Given that Festus is speaking diplomatically to a Jewish king, "religion" captures the intended register. The word literally means "fear of the divine."

7. **v.21, 25 — "the Augustus" (του Σεβαστου):** Sinaiticus uses the Greek honorific Σεβαστος, the equivalent of Latin "Augustus." Rendered as "the Augustus" to preserve the titular quality of the term, distinguishing it from the personal name "Caesar" (Καισαρ) used elsewhere in the passage.

8. **v.23 — "audience hall" (ακροατηριον):** A technical term for the formal hearing room in the Roman governor's residence. Preserved as "audience hall" rather than the more generic "hearing room" to reflect the ceremonial grandeur described by Luke (μετα πολλης φαντασιας, "with great pomp").

9. **v.26 — "my lord" (τω κυριω):** Festus refers to Caesar as κυριος ("lord"). This is the imperial usage of the title — a political designation distinct from the christological use of κυριος throughout Acts. The ambiguity is theologically charged: Luke's readers would recognize that the true κυριος is Christ, not Caesar.

10. **Textual note — Sinaiticus orthography:** Several Sinaiticus-specific spellings appear in this passage (e.g., itacistic vowel interchanges). These represent phonetic spelling conventions of the fourth-century scribe and do not affect translation. The underlying text is translated as intended.

— LOGOS
